perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include information required to be disclosed in a judicial or administrative proceeding, or otherwise required to be disclosed by law or regulation, although the requirements of paragraph 6 hereof shall apply prior to any disclosure being made.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include information required to be disclosed in a judicial or administrative proceeding, or otherwise required to be disclosed by law or regulation, and paragraph 6 hereof shall not apply prior to any disclosure being made.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause indicating that paragraph 6 applies, the NDA now contradicts the standard process for disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 90.502(2) outlines procedures for asserting attorney-client privilege, which involves protecting confidential communications even when disclosure is compelled. Removing the requirement for paragraph 6 to apply undermines the duty to protect privileged information, potentially violating the statute.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title VII > Chapter 90 > Section 502 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title VII EVIDENCE Chapter 90 EVIDENCE CODE Entire Chapter SECTION 502 Lawyer-client privilege. F.S. 90.502 90.502 Lawyer-client privilege. — (1) For purposes of this section: (a) A “lawyer” is a person authorized, or reasonably believed by the client to be authorized, to practice law in any state or nation. (b) A “client” is any person, public officer, corporation, association, or other organization or entity, either public or private, who consults a lawyer with the purpose of obtaining legal services or who is rendered legal services by a lawyer. (c) A communication between lawyer and client is “confidential” if it is not intended to be disclosed to third persons other than: 1. Those to whom disclosure is in furtherance of the rendition of legal services to the client. 2. Those reasonably necessary for the transmission of the communication. (2) A client has a privilege to refuse to disclose, and to prevent any other person from disclosing, the contents of confidential communications when such other person learned of the communications because they were made in the rendition of legal services to the client. (3) The privilege may be claimed by: (a) The client. (b) A guardian or conservator of the client. (c) The personal representative of a deceased client. (d) A successor, assignee, trustee in dissolution, or any similar representative of an organization, corporation, or association or other entity, either public or private, whether or not in existence. (e) The lawyer, but only on behalf of the client. The lawyer’s authority to claim the privilege is presumed in the absence of contrary evidence. (4) There is no lawyer-client privilege under this section when: (a) The services of the lawyer were sought or obtained to enable or aid anyone to commit or plan to commit what the client knew was a crime or fraud. (b) A communication is relevant to an issue between parties who claim through the same deceased client. (c) A communication is relevant to an issue of breach of duty by the lawyer to the client or by the client to the lawyer, arising from the lawyer-client relationshi
perturbation.justification: The change removes a clause requiring paragraph 6 to apply before disclosure of confidential information in legal proceedings. The Law Explanation states this undermines the duty to protect privileged information under Florida Statute 90.502(2). Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Florida Statute 90.502, defines lawyer-client privilege and the client's right to prevent disclosure of confidential communications made during legal services. Removing the requirement for paragraph 6 to apply directly contradicts the client's right to protect these communications, making the contract non-compliant with the statute, thus creating a legal contradiction as the altered text weakens legal protections.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations and restrictions imposed by this Agreement will not apply to any information that: a. The Recipient can demonstrate was already known to the Recipient prior to the disclosure by the Discloser; or, b. has become publicly known through no wrongful act of the Recipient; or, c. was received by the Recipient without breach of this Agreement from a third party without restriction as to the use and disclosure of the DiVclRVeU¶V Confidential Information; or, d. was independently developed by the Recipient without use of the DiVclRVeU¶V Confidential Information; or e. was ordered to be publicly released by the requirement of a government agency. In this regard, the Parties understand that the Discloser iV VXbjecW WR FlRUida¶V PXblic RecRUdV AcW, Chapter 119, Florida Statutes, and that section 1004.22, Florida Statutes, provides limited protection of documents received by the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations and restrictions imposed by this Agreement will not apply to any information that: a. The Recipient can demonstrate was already known to the Recipient prior to the disclosure by the Discloser; or, b. has become publicly known through no wrongful act of the Recipient; or, c. was received by the Recipient without breach of this Agreement from a third party without restriction as to the use and disclosure of the DiVclRVeU¶V Confidential Information; or, d. was independently developed by the Recipient without use of the DiVclRVeU¶V Confidential Information; or e. was ordered to be publicly released by the requirement of a government agency. In this regard, the Parties understand that the Discloser is NOT subject to Florida's Public Records Act, Chapter 119, Florida Statutes, and that section 1004.22, Florida Statutes, provides limited protection of documents received by the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Stating the Discloser is NOT subject to Florida's Public Records Act directly contradicts the reality for JEA, a publicly owned utility, and creates a false premise for handling confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Chapter 119, Florida Statutes - Public Records Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Chapter 119, Florida Statutes, mandates public access to government records. Asserting that the Discloser (JEA) is not subject to this law directly violates the statute's core principle, which requires government transparency.

perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the Discloser (JEA) is 'NOT subject to Florida's Public Records Act, Chapter 119, Florida Statutes'. This directly contradicts the general policy outlined in Chapter 119, which mandates public access to government records. The original text acknowledges that the Discloser is subject to this act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that Chapter 119 pertains to 'PUBLIC RECORDS' and includes sections such as 'General state policy on public records' and 'Inspection and copying of records; photographing public records; fees; exemptions.' By explicitly stating that the Discloser is NOT subject to this act, the changed text creates a direct and impactful contradiction of the law, as it seeks to exempt JEA from a law that is generally applicable to it.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Parties’ duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall remain in effect indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Parties’ duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall expire at the end of the term.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'indefinitely' to 'at the end of the term' removes the perpetual obligation to protect confidential information, contradicting the typical expectation in NDAs and trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 688.002 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. Limiting the confidentiality obligation to the term length of the agreement may not adequately protect trade secrets beyond that period, potentially violating the intent of the act.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the intent of trade secret protection, as explained in the LLM Law Explanation, and aligns with the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition because the term 'indefinitely' is misaligned with 'at the end of the term'. Florida Statute 688.002(4) defines a "trade secret" as information that derives economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The original text ensures indefinite protection, while the changed text limits this protection to the term of the agreement, potentially undermining the 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' requirement if the information retains its value beyond the term.

perturbation.original_text: The Federal and state courts located in Duval County, Florida shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to resolve all disputes arising under this agreement through binding arbitration. The location of the arbitration shall be determined at the sole discretion of the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Switching from court jurisdiction to mandatory arbitration, especially with the Discloser choosing the location, potentially limits the Recipient's legal recourse and fair process, moving disputes outside the normal court system
perturbation.law_explanation: While Florida law permits arbitration agreements, it often requires specific language and considerations to ensure fairness, particularly regarding location and impartiality. Allowing the Discloser to unilaterally determine the arbitration location may create an imbalance of power and could be challenged as unconscionable under contract law principles that prioritize fairness and equal bargaining power.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies court jurisdiction in Duval County, Florida, for dispute resolution. The changed text mandates binding arbitration, with the Discloser choosing the arbitration location. This directly contradicts the original agreement by removing the right to resolve disputes in court and giving the Discloser undue influence over the arbitration process, raising concerns about fairness and potentially conflicting with contract law principles related to equal bargaining power. The Law Explanation specifically mentions that allowing the Discloser to unilaterally determine the arbitration location may create an imbalance of power and could be challenged as unconscionable under contract law principles that prioritize fairness and equal bargaining power.
